---
title: "Titanic — Tidymodels Tour"
subtitle: "A pedagogical walk through the Kaggle Titanic competition with R and tidymodels"
---

## Welcome aboard

This site is a guided tour of the [Kaggle **Titanic — Machine Learning from
Disaster**](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/titanic) competition, worked
end to end in R with the [tidymodels](https://www.tidymodels.org/) framework.
It is written for an R user who is comfortable with the language and with
statistics, but is **new to Kaggle** — so we narrate the *competition workflow*
as well as the modelling reasoning, and we lean on `ggplot2` for every chart.

If you have taught or sat through an intro-to-ML workshop, this will feel
familiar: split, engineer features, resample, tune, evaluate, predict. Kaggle
just adds a public leaderboard and a strict submission format on top.

### What is the Kaggle Titanic competition?

Titanic is the canonical "first Kaggle competition" — the *Hello, World* of
predictive modelling. The setup:

- You are given a **training set** (`data/raw/train.csv`, 891 passengers) where
  the outcome is known: did each passenger **survive** (`Survived` = 1) or not
  (`Survived` = 0)?
- You are given a **test set** (`data/raw/test.csv`, 418 passengers) with the
  *same features but no `Survived` column*.
- Your job: **predict `Survived` for every passenger in the test set**, write
  those predictions to a CSV in the required format, and upload it. Kaggle
  scores your CSV on accuracy against the hidden true labels and places you on
  the leaderboard.

There is no prize money here — it's a sandbox. That is exactly why it's a great
place to *have fun and explore*: try a model, submit, see your score, iterate.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The Kaggle mental model for a newcomer
Think of it as a take-home exam where you can resubmit as often as you like and
see your grade each time (with a daily submission cap). Your **cross-validation
score** on the training data is your *private estimate*; the **leaderboard
score** is the *graded result*. When the two diverge, you are learning
something about generalization — which is the whole point.
:::

## The goal

Predict the binary outcome `Survived` for the 418 test passengers as accurately
as possible, and document the reasoning so the whole thing is reproducible and
teachable.

We fit **three** different model families on identical features so they can be
compared apples-to-apples:

| Model | Engine | Why it's interesting |
|-------|--------|----------------------|
| [XGBoost](docs/02-xgboost.qmd) | `xgboost` | Gradient-boosted trees — the Kaggle workhorse |
| [Random Forest](docs/03-random-forest.qmd) | `ranger` | Bagged trees — robust, low-tuning baseline |
| [Neural Net](docs/04-neural-net.qmd) | `brulee` / `nnet` | A small MLP — needs normalized inputs |

## How the documents link together

This is a small Quarto **website**. `index.qmd` (this page) is the master
document; everything else lives in `docs/` and the navbar ties them into a
narrative:

```
Home (index)  →  EDA  →  Models {XGBoost, Random Forest, Neural Net}  →  Submission  →  Kaggle Setup
```

- **[Exploratory Data Analysis](docs/01-eda.qmd)** — look at the data, find the
  patterns (sex, class, age, family, title), and *motivate* the feature
  engineering.
- **Model docs** — each one follows the *same* structure (recipe → spec →
  workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save), so once you've read one, the
  others are easy to skim for the differences.
- **[Submission](docs/submission.qmd)** — a single *parameterized* document that
  turns any of the three saved models into a leaderboard-ready CSV.
- **[Kaggle Setup](docs/setup-kaggle.md)** — how to authenticate the Kaggle CLI
  so you can download the data and push submissions.

## Project structure

```
titantic/
├── _quarto.yml              # website config (theme, navbar, freeze)
├── index.qmd                # ← you are here (master document)
├── renv.lock                # pinned package versions (reproducibility)
├── R/
│   ├── 00_setup.R           # libraries, seed=42, paths, parallel backend
│   ├── 01_eda.R             # standalone EDA script
│   └── 02_features.R        # make_recipe(data) — the SHARED feature recipe
├── data/
│   └── raw/
│       ├── train.csv        # 891 rows, has Survived
│       └── test.csv         # 418 rows, no Survived
├── docs/
│   ├── 01-eda.qmd           # exploratory analysis
│   ├── 02-xgboost.qmd       # boosted trees
│   ├── 03-random-forest.qmd # ranger random forest
│   ├── 04-neural-net.qmd    # brulee/nnet MLP
│   ├── submission.qmd       # parameterized: build a submission CSV
│   └── setup-kaggle.md      # Kaggle CLI auth guide
├── models/                  # fitted workflows (*_fit.rds) + submission_*.csv
└── figures/                 # exported plots
```

The single most important shared piece is **`R/02_features.R`**, which defines
`make_recipe(data)`. Every model sources it so they all train on the *same*
engineered features — that is what makes the comparison fair.

## How to reproduce

Everything is pinned with [`renv`](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/) and uses
`here::here()` for paths, so there is no `setwd()` fragility.

1. **Restore the environment** (installs the exact package versions):

   ```r
   renv::restore()
   ```

2. **Get the data** — follow [the Kaggle setup guide](docs/setup-kaggle.md) to
   authenticate, then download `train.csv` and `test.csv` into `data/raw/`.

3. **Render the site.** Because of `freeze: auto`, the heavy model fits only run
   the first time (or when a model doc changes):

   ```bash
   quarto render
   ```

   Logical run order if you prefer to step through it: EDA → each model doc
   (which saves its fitted workflow to `models/`) → submission.

4. **Generate submissions** for each model (see below).

::: {.callout-note}
`set.seed(42)` is set in `R/00_setup.R` and the resampling folds are seeded, so
CV results are reproducible across machines that share the `renv` lockfile.
:::

## Results summary

The table below compares the three models on **5-fold cross-validated**
`accuracy` and `roc_auc` (positive class = `"1"`, survived). These are *honest*
estimates computed on the training data before we ever touch the test set.

| Model | CV accuracy | CV ROC-AUC | Notes |
|-------|------------:|-----------:|-------|
| XGBoost | _TBD_ | _TBD_ | tuned `trees`, `tree_depth`, `learn_rate`, `min_n` |
| Random Forest | _TBD_ | _TBD_ | tuned `mtry`, `min_n`; 1000 trees |
| Neural Net | _TBD_ | _TBD_ | tuned `hidden_units`, `penalty`, `epochs`; normalized inputs |

::: {.callout-important}
This table is a **placeholder**. Fill the cells in after you render the model
documents — each one prints its `collect_metrics()` summary that you can copy
here. (A nice exercise: write a small chunk that reads the three `*_fit.rds`
files and assembles this table automatically.)
:::

## The submission workflow at a glance

Once a model is fitted and saved (each model doc does this), turning it into a
leaderboard entry is mechanical:

1. Load the fitted workflow from `models/<model>_fit.rds`.
2. Read `data/raw/test.csv`.
3. `predict()` the class for each test passenger.
4. Write `models/submission_<model>.csv` with exactly two columns,
   `PassengerId` and `Survived` (0/1 integers).
5. Push it to Kaggle:

   ```bash
   kaggle competitions submit -c titanic \
     -f models/submission_xgboost.csv \
     -m "xgboost via tidymodels"
   ```

All of that is wrapped in the **[parameterized submission document](docs/submission.qmd)**,
so you generate any model's CSV with one command:

```bash
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:xgboost
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:rf
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:nnet
```

## Next steps

- Start with the **[Exploratory Data Analysis](docs/01-eda.qmd)** to see the
  patterns that drive the features.
- Then read the model docs in order:
  **[XGBoost](docs/02-xgboost.qmd)** →
  **[Random Forest](docs/03-random-forest.qmd)** →
  **[Neural Net](docs/04-neural-net.qmd)**.
- Build a leaderboard CSV with the **[Submission](docs/submission.qmd)** doc.
- Stuck on authentication? See the **[Kaggle Setup guide](docs/setup-kaggle.md)**.
